name: military_pfas_plume_municipal_intake_blend
premise: Water commissioner Casey Bennet must decide whether to blend downriver intake during a PFAS plume tied to an upstream Army fire-training site. Army environmental lead Dana Ortega offers funded carbon filters if the city keeps intakes open to preserve pressure for wildfire tanker drafting. Shutting intakes switches to limited groundwater that violates drought permits within days. Blending may keep taps flowing but elevates lifetime exposure for bottle-fed infants above voluntary health goals.

agents.name: Casey Bennet
agents.identity: I am the municipal water commissioner accountable to EPA advisories, infant health advocates, and regional wildfire mutual aid agreements.
agents.goal: I want PFAS minimized at the tap without leaving tanker aircraft without drafting pressure during red-flag wind.

agents.name: Dana Ortega
agents.identity: I am the installation environmental lead coordinating funded remediation, interim filters, and interagency communications about training legacy contaminants.
agents.goal: I want cities to accept interim blends with filters because abrupt intake closure could collapse mutual-aid pressure during the wind event tonight.

choices: I will close river intakes and breach drought variance with signed emergency groundwater draws, accepting permit fights and lower drafting pressure until portable pumps arrive.
I will accept Army-funded filters and authorize controlled blending under infant bottled-water advisory and daily PFAS testing, accepting prolonged low-level exposure debates.
forced_choice.call_to_action: Red-flag winds start at dusk and infant health groups are filing for injunction. Casey Bennet must choose between intake closure with groundwater variance or filtered blending. What does Casey Bennet do?
